name: BayCom Corp

profile.description: BayCom Corp is the bank holding company for United Business Bank, a community-focused commercial bank providing loans, deposits, and related financial services to small and mid-sized businesses, service professionals, and individuals. The bank operates through full-service branches across Northern and Southern California, Las Vegas, Denver, Seattle, and Central New Mexico, specializing in commercial real estate, C&I lending, and SBA loans.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): BayCom has grown through disciplined acquisitions (formerly Bay Commercial Bank, renamed in 2017, IPO in 2018) and geographic expansion across the Western U.S., but no transformative product launches or business model pivots in the last five years — the company remains a traditional community bank [1]. The April 2026 leadership transition to former PacWest executives signals a strategic inflection toward larger transactions, but this is prospective rather than proven. AI Deployment (0/2): No evidence of AI-powered products or quantified AI-driven cost savings. United Business Bank offers standard digital banking services (online/mobile banking, remote deposit capture, ACH origination) without AI differentiation [1]. Market Share (1/2): United Business Bank is a meaningful player in Western U.S. community banking with $2.6B in total assets and $2.0B in loans, serving small and mid-sized businesses across seven states, but is not a category leader or fastest share gainer [1]. Pipeline (0/2): No specific next-generation products or technology platforms announced with launch dates or revenue visibility. The new CEO Christopher Baron's growth strategy is articulated at a high level but lacks specific product pipeline detail [2]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Community banks generally invest at or above peer averages in technology infrastructure relative to their asset base, and BayCom's digital banking capabilities are current for a bank of its size.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): United Business Bank positions itself as a community-focused commercial bank serving small and mid-sized businesses across the Western U.S., emphasizing relationship-based banking and local market expertise [3]. This identifies what the bank does and for whom, but is not distinctive — it could describe any community bank. The mission doesn't articulate a deeper purpose beyond serving business customers. Community Impact (1/2): As a community bank, United Business Bank has CRA (Community Reinvestment Act) obligations and provides lending services to small businesses and underserved communities across its seven-state footprint [3]. The bank offers SBA loans and products that support small business access to capital, though specific charitable programs, foundation activity, or community investment dollar amounts are not publicly detailed. Employee Retention (0/2): No specific retention data, tenure metrics, or employee satisfaction scores available. The April 2026 leadership transition — terminating the founding CEO, COO, and CFO simultaneously — suggests organizational disruption that may affect employee morale and trust [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Recent insider transactions show purchases at $30.94 and $29.99-30.22 per share, indicating some insider conviction at current price levels near book value [4].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: BayCom's financials show a well-capitalized community bank with improving profitability. Q1 2026 EPS of $0.75 (+43.5% YoY) driven by a $2.3M increase in net interest income demonstrates strong earnings momentum [1]. Profit margin of 26.4% is healthy for a community bank. ROE of 7.8% is below the 15% target but typical for conservatively capitalized banks trading near book value (P/B 0.99) [4]. Forward P/E of 11.19x is attractive, and revenue grew 15.8% YoY. The bank maintains $305.9M in cash against $20.4M in debt (D/E data unavailable from yfinance for banks). Dividend yield of 3.81% with rapidly growing payouts ($0.20/share in 2022 to $1.20 annualized in 2026, 65% CAGR) shows shareholder return commitment, though only 4 years of payment history limits the dividend metric contribution. Payout ratio of 43.4% is sustainable. 3-year revenue CAGR is minimal at 0.15% (2022 $100.9M to 2025 $101.4M), reflecting stable but not growing net interest income prior to the recent acceleration. Liquidity floor cleared: 55K avg vol x $31.55 = $1.74M daily [4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Community banking is partially protected from AI disruption — relationship-based lending to small and mid-sized businesses relies on local market knowledge, face-to-face relationships, and complex credit underwriting that AI cannot fully replicate [1]. Commercial real estate lending, SBA loans, and C&I credit require human judgment on borrower quality, local market conditions, and deal structuring. However, AI poses meaningful efficiency pressure: fintech competitors are automating loan origination, credit decisioning, and customer service. AI-powered banks and neobanks could compress margins in standardized lending products. BayCom's smaller scale (327 employees, $2.6B assets) makes it more vulnerable to technology-driven competition than larger banks with bigger IT budgets. AI Opportunity (1/1): While BayCom does not generate direct AI product revenue, the bank deploys standard digital banking tools including online/mobile banking, automated clearing house origination, and remote deposit capture that leverage modern technology infrastructure to serve business customers efficiently [1].

infinite_game.summary: BayCom shows mixed infinite game characteristics. The bank has a 22-year operating history (incorporated 2004) and has grown through disciplined acquisitions. However, the April 2026 leadership transition — simultaneously terminating the founding CEO, COO, and CFO to install former PacWest executives — represents an abrupt organizational disruption that contradicts infinite game principles of trusting teams and leadership continuity. The new team's focus on 'transformational transactions' and 'high-performance culture' uses finite game language focused on dealmaking rather than enduring purpose.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): BayCom's purpose — providing banking services to small and mid-sized businesses across the Western U.S. — is genuine but shallow [3]. Community banking serves a real need (small business access to capital), but BayCom's articulation of this purpose is interchangeable with any community bank. The April 2026 leadership transition emphasized 'building a balance sheet, navigating capital markets, and executing larger transactions' rather than advancing a cause [2]. The new team's language is transactional rather than purposeful.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): The April 2026 leadership transition is a significant red flag for organizational trust. The board terminated founding CEO George Guarini, COO Janet King, and CFO Keary Colwell simultaneously 'without cause,' replacing all three with former PacWest Bancorp executives [2]. This wholesale leadership replacement — removing the founders who built the bank from its 2004 inception — signals a low-trust governance dynamic. Employees who joined because of the founding team's vision now face uncertainty under an entirely new leadership structure focused on a different strategic direction. No employee satisfaction or retention data is available to assess the cultural impact.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): BayCom's board and new leadership implicitly acknowledge competitive dynamics by hiring former PacWest executives — recognizing that larger banks' capabilities in capital markets, deal execution, and balance sheet management represent strengths that BayCom needs to develop [2]. The strategic shift toward 'targeted growth model' and 'transformational transactions' reflects learning from competitors' strengths rather than attempting to destroy them.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): BayCom has demonstrated incremental strategic evolution — growing from a single-market community bank to a seven-state platform through acquisitions, completing an IPO in 2018, and now pivoting toward larger transformational deals under new leadership [2]. The April 2026 leadership change represents the most dramatic strategic adaptation in the company's history. However, the core business model (commercial banking) remains unchanged, and the pivot was driven by the board's growth ambitions rather than a cause-driven transformation.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): The board's April 2026 decision to replace the entire founding leadership team was a bold move — firing a founding CEO, COO, and CFO simultaneously requires conviction that a different strategic approach is needed [2]. The board concluded 'the next phase of growth requires proven experience building a balance sheet, navigating capital markets, and executing larger, more transformational transactions' — a candid assessment of the founding team's limitations. However, this decision was driven by financial ambition (growth and dealmaking) rather than principled adherence to a Just Cause.

investment_thesis: BayCom Corp offers a compelling community bank value story trading at 0.99x book value and 11.19x forward P/E with accelerating earnings growth — Q1 2026 EPS of $0.75 (+43.5% YoY) and FY2025 EPS of $2.42 (+47% YoY) demonstrate strong momentum driven by expanding net interest income [1][4]. The bank's $2.6B asset base, $2.0B loan portfolio, and 3.81% dividend yield (growing at 65% CAGR over 3 years) provide attractive income and value characteristics. The April 2026 leadership transition to former PacWest executives signals a strategic pivot toward larger deal-making and scale-building that could unlock significant shareholder value if the new team executes on acquisition-driven growth across Western U.S. markets [2]. The Inspire Impact score of 34 (ALIGNED) with positive attributions for Ethical Labor Practices, Hiring Ethics, and Product Access and Affordability reflects the community banking mission of serving small business lending needs.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on leadership uncertainty, limited scale, and thin liquidity. The simultaneous termination of the founding CEO, COO, and CFO in April 2026 creates significant execution risk — new CEO Christopher Baron and his PacWest team are inheriting a 327-employee bank and must rebuild management culture, strategy, and relationships from scratch [2]. BayCom is a micro-cap ($344M market cap) with average daily volume of only 55K shares ($1.74M daily), barely clearing the IOWN liquidity floor — institutional investors may struggle to build or exit positions. ROE of 7.8% is below community bank peers, and the 3-year revenue CAGR of 0.2% indicates the legacy business has been stagnant before the recent earnings acceleration [4]. Commercial real estate concentration risk is elevated, with CRE representing the majority of the $2.0B loan portfolio in markets (California, Colorado, Nevada) that face potential valuation pressure. The bank has no competitive technology moat against fintech competitors or larger banks with greater AI and digital investment budgets.
key_catalysts: New leadership team of former PacWest executives (CEO Christopher Baron, EVP William Black, CFO Kevin Thompson) brings proven experience in capital markets and transformational M&A that could accelerate growth from the $2.6B asset base [2]
Q1 2026 EPS of $0.75 (+43.5% YoY) with $2.3M net interest income expansion signals accelerating profitability trajectory, with forward EPS of $2.82 implying continued strong earnings growth [1][4]
Trading at 0.99x book value with 3.81% dividend yield and 65% dividend growth CAGR provides downside protection and growing income stream as new leadership pursues scale-building acquisitions [4]
key_risks: Wholesale leadership transition in April 2026 — simultaneous termination of founding CEO, COO, and CFO creates execution risk as the new PacWest team must rebuild strategy, culture, and customer relationships across seven states [2]

Micro-cap ($344M) with average daily volume of only 55K shares ($1.74M daily), barely clearing the liquidity floor — thin trading limits institutional participation and creates exit risk during market stress [4]

Commercial real estate concentration risk in California, Nevada, and Colorado markets with potential CRE valuation pressure, and no meaningful technology or AI competitive moat against larger banks and fintech competitors [1]
sources: BayCom Corp Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 Earnings Releases — $6.9M Q4 2025 net income, $8.2M Q1 2026 net income, $0.75 EPS (+43.5% YoY), $2.6B total assets, January and April 2026
BayCom Corp Leadership Transition Announcement — CEO George Guarini terminated, Christopher Baron appointed CEO, William Black EVP, Kevin Thompson CFO, April 2026
United Business Bank Website, CRA and Community Banking Services — relationship banking, SBA loans, small business lending, accessed June 2026
